Crafting Framework & Oddities Brainstorm

The Core Concept

The idea is to take a Base Item (like those found in 33_Equipment.md), gather Scrap (just a generic, stackable resource), and tape Oddities (unique bits of junk that provide specific traits) onto it.

Your Brain Limits Your Chaos

  • Brains Stat & Utility Slots: As noted in 10_Stats.md, your Brains score gives you “Utility Slots.” These slots represent your capacity to manage, maintain, and wield dangerously complex contraptions.
  • Rule Suggestion: An item can have a number of attached Oddities up to a maximum equal to the wielder’s Brains. If a Goblin with Brains 1 tries to pick up a weapon that has 3 attached Oddities, they can’t figure out how to pull the triggering mechanisms and it acts as a clumsy paperweight.

The True Cost: Quality Degradation

To balance the chaos of taping a “Fiery” oddity to a “Bouncy” oddity to a Heavy Hammer, we weaponize the Quality mechanic (33_Equipment.md).

  • The Baseline: A “Goblin Made” item breaks on a roll of 1 on the gear dice.
  • The Oddity Tax: Each Oddity attached to a weapon increases the “Break Chance” threshold by 1 on the die.
    • 0 Oddities: Breaks on a 1.
    • 1 Oddity: Breaks on 1-2.
    • 2 Oddities: Breaks on 1-3.
    • 3 Oddities: Breaks on 1-4.
  • Why this works: It creates an incredible burst of temporary power. The player gets a super-weapon, but it is statistically guaranteed to blow up in their hands soon. It balances itself through imminent destruction, forcing the player to scavenge again.

GM Scaffolding Framework

Players will combine things you didn’t anticipate. Here is a 3-step scaffold for the GM (the “Rules Lawyer”) to adjudicate any combination on the fly.

Step 1: Determine the Base Skeleton Always start with the underlying item. This provides the fundamental stats (Bulk, Handedness, base Damage output). e.g., A “Medium Weapon” (Bulk 2, One-Handed, +3d Damage).

Step 2: Apply the Tags (The Oddities) Read the Oddities tags sequentially. Do not overthink the physics. If the Oddity says “Bouncy,” the weapon now bounces. If it says “Fiery,” it sets things on fire. Let the narrative form naturally from the tags. e.g., Medium Weapon + Bouncy + Fiery = A fiery hunk of metal that bounces between targets.

Step 3: Define “The Exploit” and “The Rebound” The final step is to determine the immediate mechanical effect on a Hit vs. a Fumble/Break.

  • Hit (The Exploit): What cool thing happens? (e.g., The Bouncy Fire-Hammer hits two targets in the same zone).
  • Fumble/Break (The Rebound): What awful thing happens when it shatters? (e.g., It explodes in a fiery blast, hitting the Goblin and anyone nearby).

d66 List of Oddities

Here is a starting list of 36 distinct Oddities that players can find out in the world, categorized by their dominant feature.

d66 Oddity Name The Tag / Effect
11 Bouncy Gland Bouncy: Can be thrown into an adjacent zone and returns to hand. On a miss, it hits a random target in the zone.
12 Sticky Sap Sticky: Target hit cannot willingly move to a new zone next round.
13 Heavy Core Heavy: +1d Damage, but increases the item’s Bulk by +1.
14 Screeching Whistle Loud: Forces a morale/rout test on Mobs it hits, but breaks stealth instantly for the whole group.
15 Rusty Spring Propelled: Extends melee reach to an adjacent zone, or adds +1d to movement actions.
16 Floaty Bladder Light: Reduces the item’s Bulk by -1 (minimum 0).
21 Spark Battery Shocking: Ignores any armor based on metal. Target drops whatever they are holding.
22 Volatile Ooze Acidic: Destroys enemy armor on a critical hit, but destroys your armor if it breaks.
23 Spicy Pepper Box Blinding: Characters hit lose their passive defense dice for the round.
24 Magnetic Shard Pulling: Pulls metallic items from an adjacent zone towards you.
25 Glow Worm Jar Blazing: Illuminates the darkness. Dazzles targets in melee (+1d to hit them).
26 Chilling Frost-Stone Freezing: Extinguishes fires. Target hit loses 1 action next turn due to shivering.
31 Spiked Carapace Jagged: Rolls of 6 deal 1 bleeding wound to the target.
32 Twitchy Muscle Alive: +1d to hit, but requires a Slink test to put the item down safely without it biting you.
33 Venom Gland Toxic: Deals damage over time, ignoring Grit entirely (requires an antidote).
34 Stretchy Sinew Whip-like: Can hit two targets in your current zone with a single strike.
35 Bone-Crushing Vise Crushing: +2d Damage against targets with passive defense dice (armor), -1d against unarmored.
36 Puffball Spore Choking: Creates a cloud in the zone; all actions inside lose -1d next round.
41 Clockwork Gyro Whirling: Allows an attack against all enemies in the zone, but also hits all allies.
42 Telescoping Tube Extending: Can strike enemies anywhere in line-of-sight, but takes 1 action to retract.
43 Mirror Shard Reflective: Can bounce ranged attacks back at the attacker on a critical defense.
44 Pressure Valve Steaming: Pushes targets out of your zone into an adjacent zone on a hit.
45 Alchemical Primer Explosive: Converts single-target damage to an area-of-effect blast (entire zone), but always breaks on a 1-3.
46 Fragile Glass Hull Shattering: Does massive +3d damage, but breaks automatically after 1 successful hit.
51 Goblin-Bite Clamp Latching: Clamps onto the target. You gain +2d to all subsequent attacks against them, but you are attached to them.
52 Rat-Skull Rattle Spooky: Targets must pass a Brains test to willingly attack you in melee.
53 Thick Sludge Gooey: Absorbs impacts. If attached to armor, halves incoming damage but restricts Movement.
54 Screaming Crystal Shatter-Sonic: Deals damage directly to Mobs instead of Bosses, bypassing their human shields.
55 Hollowed Horn Amplifying: Any order given through this item gives a built-in +1d boon to your Mobs.
56 Void-Touched Rock Teleporting: Swap places with the target upon a successful hit.
61 Troll-Blood Vial Regenerating: Weapon repairs itself at the end of combat (negates one break).
62 Shiny Coin Slot Greedy: Deals +1d damage for every piece of treasure/loot you shove into it before attacking.
63 Boomerang Fin Curving: Ignores any cover the target might be hiding behind.
64 Stolen Magic Wand Erratic: Roll a d6 after every attack. On a 1, you turn pink. On a 6, the target turns into a sheep.
65 Giant’s Toenail Massive Force: Any target hit is immediately knocked prone.
66 The Red Button Panic: Does something spectacular and devastating (GM’s discretion), but breaks the item permanently and irreparably.

Enemy Stats & Combat Mechanics Draft

Context: Shifting away from GM-tracked Hit Points to a static “One-Hit Kill” threshold system for 90% of enemies, speeding up combat significantly.

Core Philosophy

The GM does not track HP for standard enemies. Goblins are lethal when they swarm, and combat should be resolved instantly. An enemy’s survivability is reflected in how hard they are to hit, not how many hits they can take.

The 5 Enemy Stats

Every standard enemy has a very simple stat block:

  1. Attack: The base physical damage they deal automatically when they attack a Gobbo.
  2. Defence (Target Number): The number of successes a Gobbo needs on an Attack roll to instantly kill this enemy.
  3. Movement: How many Zones they can cross in an action.
  4. Range: Melee (same Zone) or Ranged (X Zones).
  5. Special: A unique ability, Boon, or attack difficulty modifier (e.g., “Hard Attack” or “Creates Fire”).

Example Profiles

  • Angry Farmer:
    • Attack: 1 (Pitchfork) | Defence: 1 (Unarmored)
    • Movement: 1 | Range: Melee | Special: None
  • Human Soldier:
    • Attack: 2 (Sword) | Defence: 2 (Leather Armor/Shield)
    • Movement: 1 | Range: Melee | Special: Shield Wall (Requires +1 Success if attacked from the front).
  • Armored Knight:
    • Attack: 3 (Greatsword) | Defence: 3 (Plate Armor)
    • Movement: 1 | Range: Melee | Special: Hard Attack. The Knight is incredibly skilled; dodging their attacks is a Hard difficulty test (Success only on a 6).

How Combat Flows

Gobbo Attacks Enemy (The “One-Hit Kill”)

A player attacks the Knight (Defence 3). They roll their Tough stat for a Melee Attack.

  • If they roll 3 or more successes, their rusty spear finds a chink in the Knight’s plate armor. The Knight instantly dies.
  • If they roll 1 or 2 successes, the attack simply bounces off the armor. No effect.
  • (Note: This makes Gobbos teaming up or Mobs swarming essential for taking down high-Defence targets).

Enemy Attacks Gobbo (Dodge & Parry)

Because the GM does not roll dice, an enemy’s Attack represents guaranteed incoming damage.

When an enemy targets a Gobbo, the player must use a Reaction to survive:

  1. Dodge: The player tests Slink.
  2. Parry: The player tests Tough (requires a weapon/shield).
  • The Math: Every success the player rolls reduces the incoming Attack damage by 1.
  • Example: The Knight attacks a Gobbo. The Knight’s Attack is 3, and their Special makes dodging Hard (Success on 6s only). The Gobbo rolls 4d6 for Slink, getting two 6s (2 successes). The 3 Damage is reduced by 2. The Gobbo takes 1 Damage.

Armor

If a Gobbo wears Armor, it is a static Damage Reduction that applies after the Dodge/Parry roll.

  • Example: Same scenario as above. The Gobbo takes 1 Damage. However, the Gobbo is wearing a Bucket Helmet (Armor 1). The 1 Damage is completely negated.

Boss Monsters

What about Dragons or Raid Bosses? They use the same system, but introduce Wounds.

  • The Dragon (Defence 4, Wounds 3).
  • To deal one Wound to the Dragon, a Gobbo must roll 4 successes. The Dragon doesn’t die until it takes 3 Wounds.
  • This is the only time the GM tracks health, reserving it solely for epic encounters.

Equipment Framework Brainstorm

Refined framework focusing on high-level concepts, generic structures, and slim mechanical tracking.

Core Item Attributes

Every item in the game is defined by three primary attributes. These dictate how an item interacts with the PC and the world.

1. Bulk

How heavy, large, or unwieldy an item is. Bulk is the primary limiting factor for what a Gobbo can bring to or from a raid.

  • Capacity Limit: The total Bulk a PC can carry is determined by their Carry Capacity (derived from the Tough stat).
  • Scale: Bulk generally ranges from 0 (tiny items, lockpicks, coins) to 3+ (massive statues, heavy machinery). Most standard equipment is Bulk 1 or 2.

2. Value

Value represents the item’s worth (such as “Teeth”, “Shinies”, or trade value).

  • Acquisition & Economy: Value has its own merit. Between adventures or raids, Value is used as a currency to acquire new equipment for the PC or to outfit their Mob.
  • Progression: Surplus Value brought back to the Lair can be invested in Lair Upgrades or used to increase the Gang’s Renown.

3. Usage

Usage defines how and when an item interacts with the action economy. Standardizing this prevents confusion at the table.

  • Passive: The item provides its benefit constantly just by being worn or carried (e.g., Armor granting Passive Defence).
  • Action: Using the item costs one of the PC’s standard Actions during their turn.
  • Reaction / Free: The item can be used instantly, either outside the normal action economy or in response to a specific trigger.

The Concept of “Flair” vs “Traits”

To keep the game moving fast, standard equipment shouldn’t bog down play with complex calculations.

  • Flair: Purely narrative fluff. Flair describes how cool, dirty, or shiny an item looks. It has no mechanical weight but helps players visualize their Gobbo and roleplay.
  • Mechanical Traits: Only specific, notable items have Mechanical Traits (like “Cleave” or “Reach”). Standard equipment relies on simple dice modifiers instead.

Equipment Categories (Conceptual)

1. Weapons

Weapons are tools for violence. To keep mechanics slim, a weapon’s primary function is to modify the dice pool rather than introduce complex sub-systems.

  • Standard Mechanics: A typical weapon simply adds +1d or +2d to an attack roll.
  • Special Rules: We should avoid tacking on special rules to every weapon. Special rules (Traits) should be reserved for unique weapons or specific weapon classes (e.g., blackpowder weapons), so players don’t have to look up unique rules for a standard sword.

2. Armor and Defence

Armor keeps Gobbos alive by providing pure defensive capabilities without requiring an action to use.

  • Passive Defence: Armor provides “Passive Defence” dice. These dice are rolled automatically against incoming attacks.
  • Note on Dodge: This is distinct from “Active Dodge.” Active Dodge is an intentional action fueled by the Slink stat, whereas Passive Defence just relies on wearing the armor.
  • Ablative Concept: Armor might have a mechanic where it can be sacrificed or broken to negate massive damage, serving as a consumable life-saver. (Comment: this might be a bit complex for Mobs, but perhaps a good thing for PC)

3. Consumables

Single-use items that are highly volatile and chaotic (Brews, Shrooms, Explosives).

  • Mechanics: High impact, but often carry a risk or require a chaotic roll to see if they backfire. Used via an Action or Free/Reaction depending on the item.

4. Utility / Tools

Items designed to interact with the environment or bypass obstacles, rather than for direct combat.

  • Mechanics: Often provide flat benefits to specific tests (e.g., lockpicks turning a Normal test into an Easy test) or allow actions that would otherwise be impossible.

5. Pure Treasure

Items that exist solely to be carried out of the dungeon.

  • Mechanics: High Value, High Bulk, zero utility. Creates the classic dilemma of managing Carry Capacity versus greed.

Brainstorm: Gang Mechanics

Role: Game Engineer

The Gang serves as the persistent “Class” and progression tracker for the player. Since individual Gobbos will inevitably die (and it’s a feature, not a bug!), the Gang ensures that the player’s overall power and narrative weight in the world continues to grow.

1. What is Infamy, Really? (Formerly Renown)

Infamy is the “Level” of your Gang. It represents the Gang’s reputation, fear factor, resources, and influence within the Lair and the wider world.

  • Mechanically: It dictates the baseline competence of your next Boss. As written in 15_Death.md, a new PC gets Stat Advances based on the Infamy Level. Instead of starting completely fresh at 1,1,1,1, a high Infamy gang acts as an “XP-boost” for the next Boss (e.g., letting them start at 4,1,2,1). This gives them a running start while still allowing the player to pivot to a brand new build and style.
  • Narratively: It’s how much respect and fear you command. High Infamy means other gangs step aside when you walk through the Lair.
  • Progression: Infamy increases by bringing Loot back to the Lair and completing Gang Agendas (see below).

2. Gang Attributes & The “Gang Sheet”

If the Gang acts like a flexible Class, it needs its own identity outside of the current Boss. A Gang Sheet is a fantastic idea to anchor the player. Here are attributes a Gang could have:

Core Gang Attributes (Suggested)

Instead of rolling stats, a Gang could have a few core descriptors or “Tags” that function mechanically:

  1. The Hoard: A measure of abstract wealth and gear. When a Boss needs a specific mundane item, they can test against The Hoard to see if the Gang already stole one. Crucially, The Hoard is the starting point for outfitting your Mob before a raid—you equip them from the Hoard, and then need to get hold of more loot to buy more stuff in the Lair.
  2. Specialty (The “Class” Aspect):
    • At Gang creation, the player picks a Specialty (e.g., Sneak-Thieves, Muck-Brawlers, Weird-Tinkerers).
    • Mechanic: Any Boss from this Gang always gets +1d when doing a test related to their Specialty. This gives the Gang a distinct flavor that persists across all characters.

What goes on the Gang Sheet?

  • Gang Name / Specialty
  • Current Boss: (Who is in charge right now)
  • Infamy Level: (And XP tracker towards the next level)
  • The Hoard / Resources: (Shared pool, used to outfit the mob)
  • The Bone Pile: (List of dead Bosses, their relics, and kept talents/inspiration)
  • Elders: (List of retired Bosses and what they do)
  • Active Grudges / Agendas: (Current personal missions)

3. Elders & The “Pile of Bones”

Should they belong to one gang or the whole Lair?

Recommendation: They should primarily belong to the Gang. Here is why: You want the player to feel a sense of personal lineage and reward. If my awesome PC Grurr dies and goes to the Pile of Bones, I want my next character to benefit from Saint Grurr. If it benefits the whole Lair, it waters down the impact of my specific character’s death.

However, we can do a hybrid to enhance the Lair mechanic:

  • The Bone Pile (Dead Legends): Purely Gang-specific. These are your Patron Saints. Players draw “Inspiration” from their dead PCs, allowing the new Boss to keep a highly iconic, cool talent/trick that the old PC had (though not all of them, to maintain balance). Furthermore, tying into Crafting, a player could take a literal Bone from a dead PC and attach it to an item to create a Relic, granting an even larger, specific boon from that ancestor.
  • Elders (Retired PCs): Gang-specific, BUT they can be “spent” to unlock Lair Upgrades. An Elder sits around demanding food from your Gang, giving you a passive buff (e.g., “Better prices at the merchant”). But if you want a massive Lair Upgrade (like a new Blacksmith for everyone), you might have to assign an Elder to permanently run it. This gives players a choice: keep the Elder for personal benefit, or sacrifice their personal benefit for a Lair-wide upgrade. Name suggestion for “Pile of Bones”: The Bone Pile, The Ancestor Shrines, The Wall of Skulls.

4. Personal Missions / Quests (Gang Agendas)

Unlocking personal missions via the Gang is a brilliant way to drive raid behavior and add chaos. Let’s call them Gang Agendas or Grudges.

  • How it works: A Gang always has one active Agenda. These are specific, highly flavorful objectives that must be completed during a Raid.
  • Examples:
    • The Shiny-Snatchers Agenda: Steal something from a locked chest without fighting the guards.
    • The Muck-Brawlers Agenda: Defeat the biggest enemy in the dungeon using only improvised weapons.
    • The Weird-Tinkerers Agenda: Trigger a trap on purpose and survive.
  • The Reward: Completing an Agenda grants immediate bonus Loot, Renown XP, or even a unique Trick that can be added to the Gang’s permanent repertoire.
  • Rivalries: Agendas could also target other players! “Ensure your Gang steals more Loot than [Player B’s Gang] on this raid.” (If this fits the chaotic PvP-lite tone of the game).

Summary & Next Steps

By defining Gangs with specific Specialties, giving them a Gang Sheet to track Infamy and The Bone Pile, and injecting Gang Agendas to push raid behavior, the Gang truly becomes a persistent, highly flexible “Class.”

Do you want to solidify these attribute names and create a mock-up of what the Gang Sheet would look like? Or dive deeper into how specialties branch out?

5. Creative Genius Additions (The Spice!)

Role: Creative Genius

If we step back and look at the big picture, the Gang framework is solid, but we can turn the dial up on the chaos and “goblin flavor” even more. Here are some grand, fun, and highly flavorful ideas to expand the Gangs:

A. Gang “Turf” (Micro-Lair Customization)

The Lair is big, but your Gang needs a specific corner to call home.

  • The Idea: Beyond macro Lair Upgrades (like a Blacksmith), players can customize their Gang’s Turf.
  • Mechanic: Do you sleep in hanging rusty cages? Do you have an indoor mushroom farm that smells terrible? Your Turf grants highly specific, flavorful micro-boons. E.g., a “Stolen Chandelier” in your Turf means your Grunts are slightly braver (they feel fancy). It gives the players a mini base-building game within the larger base-building game.

B. Generational Grudges & Phobias (Organic Flaws)

Goblins learn from their mistakes… sort of.

  • The Idea: The way your last Boss died permanently affects the Gang’s psychology.
  • Mechanic: If Boss #1 gets eaten by a Troll, the Gang develops a Troll Grudge. The next Boss gets +1d when fighting Trolls, but the Mob suffers -1d on morale tests around them. The Gang evolves organically based on the hilarious failures of past sessions!

C. Gang Quirks / Hysterias

A Specialty (like Sneak-Thieves) is a benefit. A Quirk is a shared madness.

  • The Idea: Every Gang rolls for a mandatory, chaotic behavioral Quirk at creation.
  • Examples:
    • Pyromaniacs: Cannot resist throwing bombs if they have them.
    • Shiny-Blindness: Always prioritize grabbing the most reflective loot, even if it’s worthless glass, over actual gold.
    • Vocal Tics: The player must shout their orders.
  • Reward: Playing into your Gang’s Quirk when it is inconvenient grants a free Trick or Grit point for that raid. It incentivizes players to act like unhinged goblins.

D. Inter-Gang Rivalries (The Lair is a Powder Keg)

The players are ostensibly on the same side, but goblins are petty.

  • The Idea: Formalize the friction between the players’ Gangs back at the Lair.
  • Mechanic: If The Sneak-Thieves and The Muck-Brawlers both want the same Lair Upgrade or the last shiny sword in the shop, they can use their Infamy to “Flex” on each other. Or perhaps they can place Wagers before a raid: “Whoever brings back the most teeth gets the other Gang to clean out their mushroom farm for a week.”

E. The Mob Mutiny Gauge

As Infamy grows, so does the ego of your Grunts.

  • The Idea: High Infamy is great, but Grunts who think they are part of a “legendary” gang expect to be treated like it (more food, more loot).
  • Mechanic: If the Boss fails too many Orders or hoarding goes poorly, the Grunts might demand a new Boss. This creates a funny tension where the Boss has to constantly impress their own followers to avoid a hostile (and violent) workplace transition!

Generic Trick Mechanics Framework

This document takes the mechanical concepts of Tricks and distills them into their raw, generic mathematical and rule-bending templates. These can be used as “formulas” to quickly construct specific Tricks by plugging in different Stats, Conditions, or Equipment.

1. Boons & Dice Manipulation

Formulas that alter the raw dice probabilities.

  • Static Bonus: Gain +X d6 on [Specific Action / Stat / Skill].
  • Contextual Bonus: Gain +X d6 on [Action / Stat] when [Specific Condition is met].
  • Enemy Penalty: Enemies suffer -X d6 when [Specific Trigger happens].
  • Target Adjustment: Treat a roll of [Number/Face] as [Better Number/Success] on [Specific Action].
  • Exploding Dice: If you roll [Specific Number] on [Action], roll 1 additional die.
  • Reroll Allocation: Reroll up to [X] failed dice on [Specific Test / Condition].
  • Die Discard: Discard the lowest die result from your pool instead of counting it, when [Condition].
  • Success Multiplier: On [Action], a roll of [Specific Number] counts as [X] successes instead of 1.
  • Critical Shift: Rolls of [Number], in addition to being a success, also inflict [Status Effect].
  • Fail Forward: On a roll with zero successes for [Action], automatically gain [Resource or Temporary Bonus].
  • Success Share: If you generate [X] more successes than required for [Test], grant [Bonus] to [Ally / Mob].

2. Economy Bending (Actions & Time)

Formulas that bend the rules of turn phases, action economy, and initiative.

  • Conditional Extra Action: Gain 1 additional [Action Type] when [Extremely Specific Condition].
  • Action Chaining: Perform [Action A] as a Free Action immediately after succeeding at [Action B].
  • Reaction Action: When [Enemy Action / Trigger] occurs, you may immediately perform [Specific Action] as a Reaction.
  • Action Downgrade: Perform [Standard Action] as a [Free Action / Cheaper Action] under [Condition].
  • Initiative Shift: You may choose to take your turn immediately before [Enemy Type / Event] acts.
  • Action Investment: If you forgo [Action Type] this turn, gain [Bonus / Action] on your next turn.
  • Speed Override: Performing [Action] takes [Less Time / Fewer Actions] than the standard rules dictate.

3. Rule Breaking & Bypassing

Formulas that let a Boss safely ignore the negative constraints that govern normal characters.

  • Auto-Pass / Exemption: You automatically succeed at [Specific Test / Hazard] without needing to roll.
  • Stat Swapping: You may roll [Stat A] instead of [Stat B] when performing [Specific Action].
  • Trait Ignorance: Ignore the [Negative Trait] rule on [Equipment / Weapon / Armor].
  • Constraint Breaking: Bypass [Specific Usage Rule] for [Equipment] (e.g., wielding a 2-handed item in 1 hand).
  • Environmental Bypass: Ignore [Specific Hazard / Terrain Penalty / Cover] when resolving [Action / Move].
  • Condition Immunity: You can never be afflicted by [Specific Status Effect / Condition].
  • Scale / Size Adjustment: You count as [Larger / Smaller] for the specific purpose of [Grappling / Carrying / Line of Sight].

4. Resource Manipulation (Grit, Grunt, Costs)

Formulas that alter the economy of health, command points, or other meta-currencies.

  • Capacity Increase: Gain +X to Max [Resource Type].
  • Cost Reduction: Reduce the activation cost of [Specific Action / Order] by [X] Resource (minimum 1).
  • Resource for Bonus: Spend [X] Resource to instantly gain +Y d6 on [Specific Action].
  • Resource for Mitigation: Spend [X] Resource to reduce incoming [Damage / Consequence] by [Y].
  • Resource Refund: Regain [X] Resource whenever [Trigger / Success Condition] occurs.
  • Desperation Buff: While at exactly [Number] Resource, gain [Specific Bonus].
  • Auto-Success via Resource: Spend [X] Resource to automatically pass [Specific Test / Morale].
  • Resource Conversion: Take [X] Damage to Resource A to instantly gain [Y] of Resource B.
  • Rest Enhancement: Regain +[X] additional Resource whenever you [Rest / Recover].

5. Mob & Leadership

Formulas relating exclusively to the interaction with and command of Mobs.

  • Damage Reassignment: Redirect [X] damage you take to a Grunt in your Mob under [Condition].
  • Mob Stat Boost: Mobs you lead gain +[X] to their base [Stat / Speed / Damage] under [Condition].
  • Mob Boon: Mobs you lead gain +[X] d6 when performing [Specific Action].
  • Mob Penalty Negation: Mobs you lead ignore [Specific Penalty] (e.g., half-size penalties).
  • Free Command: Issue [Specific Order] without spending an Action or Grunt under [Condition].
  • Casualty Mitigation: Mobs you lead ignore the first casualty they suffer from [Specific Source].
  • Mob Recovery Shortcut: [Action / Resource] automatically rallies or reforms Mobs in [Area].
  • Mob Utility Boost: Mobs you lead gain [Utility Rule] (e.g., extra carry slots, providing better cover).
  • Sacrificial Boost: Command a Mob to gain +[X] Bonus, but they automatically suffer [Y] Casualty / Penalty afterwards.

6. Survivability & Defense

Formulas that prevent a Boss from dying to stray arrows or unlucky rolls.

  • Damage Ceiling: You cannot take more than [X] damage from a single [Source / Attack].
  • Damage Type Mitigation: Reduce or ignore [Damage / Effects] from [Specific Damage Type].
  • Death Defy: When reduced to 0 [Health Resource], roll [X] d6. On a [Target Number]+, stay at 1.
  • Vampirism: Regain [X] Health Resource whenever you [Defeat Enemy / Trigger Condition].
  • Last Stand: When reduced to 0 [Health Resource], you may immediately perform [Specific Action] before dying.
  • Fast Healing: Automatically regain [X] Health Resource when [Trigger, e.g., combat starts].

7. Loot & Gear Interaction

Formulas based on manipulating items in the world.

  • Guaranteed Loot: When rolling for Loot after [Condition], automatically find at least [X] of [Item Type].
  • Field Repair: You can repair [Item Type] using [Alternative Resource / Action].
  • Carry Capacity: You can hold +[X] items of [Bulk / Specific Type] without suffering encumbrance.
  • Equipment Juggler: Swap or equip [Item Type] as a [Free Action / Cheaper Action].
  • Effect Area Increase: [Item Type, e.g., grenades] have their Area of Effect increased by [X] zones.

Brainstorm: Initial Trick List

(Moved from 14_Tricks.md so the core rulebook stays clean. This is an idea dump for future Trick tables).

General Tricks

Available to any Boss. No prerequisites.

  • Coward’s Luck
    • [Prerequisite] None | [Cost] 1 Grunt
    • Effect: After you roll the dice for any test, you may spend 1 Grunt to completely reroll all dice that did not land on a 1 (You cannot reroll Fumbles).
  • Iron Stomach
    • [Prerequisite] None | [Cost] Passive
    • Effect: You can “carry” up to 2 Bulk worth of items by swallowing them. They do not count toward your Carry Capacity. It requires 1 Action to regurgitate them.
  • Slippery
    • [Prerequisite] None | [Cost] Passive
    • Effect: Enemies do not get free reactionary Opportunity Attacks against you when you leave their Zone.

Tough Tricks

Requires physical endurance and brutality.

  • Meat Shield
    • [Prerequisite] Tough 3 | [Cost] 1 Grunt
    • Effect: When you take a Wound from an attack, you can spend 1 Grunt to instantly shove an allied Mob into the way. The Mob takes the damage instead (instantly killing 1d3 Gobbos in that Mob), and you take 0 Wounds.
  • Butcher
    • [Prerequisite] Tough 2 | [Cost] Passive
    • Effect: Gain +1 Boon Die when attacking an enemy whose Size is strictly smaller than yours.
  • Hard Head
    • [Prerequisite] Tough 4 | [Cost] Passive
    • Effect: Ignore the first non-magical Wound you would take during a Raid.

Slink Tricks

Requires agility and dirty fighting.

  • Ankle-Biter
    • [Prerequisite] Slink 3 | [Cost] Passive
    • Effect: Whenever you successfully Dodge an attack, you may immediately make a free melee attack against the attacker’s legs (if they are in your Zone).
  • Knee-Capper
    • [Prerequisite] Slink 2 | [Cost] Passive
    • Effect: Gain +1 Boon Die when attacking an enemy whose Size is strictly larger than yours.

Mouth Tricks

Requires vocal volume and a complete lack of empathy.

  • Inspiring Death
    • [Prerequisite] Mouth 3 | [Cost] Passive
    • Effect: Regain 2 Grunt instantly whenever an allied Mob in your Zone is completely wiped out by an enemy (showing your remaining gobbos what happens when they fail you).

Brains Tricks

Requires weird logic and a fascination with shiny junk.

  • (Brains Tricks TBD alongside Lair/Crafting systems)

The Lair Phase, Raid Tally and Base Upgrades

The Lair is the home base, here is where the Gobbos need to return after every advanture or raid. In the Lair, the Gobbos can heal, rest, and upgrade their gear. It is mostly a safe space, but not always.

The Lair Phase is not a specific time, it can be anything from a few days to weeks, depending on the game, and what the Gobbos trying to achive. As the world do not stop, there might sometimes be a sense of urgency. For instance if they managed to not completly finish of their last raid, they want to return fast before the surviouirs have left with the loot or perhaps gotten reinforcments.

Homecoming

At the return of every raid, the Gobbos back home are keen to hear the tales from the raid, and see all the shiny loot they brought back.

Casualties

If a PC died during the raid there is a small cermony to add the fallen to the Bone Pile. The PC will then go to the Character Creation and create a new PC.

Raid Tally

The Raid Points are calculated from the last raid, and converted back to XP according to the table below. Each PC participating in the raid, and still alive get the XP listed. Yes, you need to survive to get XP. (TDB: Is this too punishing?). Raid points cannot be saved, and any excess points are lost. So if you have 6 Raid Points you get 2 XP, and the remaining 2 Raid Points are lost.

(TDB: Is this amount reasonable?)

Raid Points XP
1-4 1 XP
5-9 2 XP
10-15 3 XP
16+ 4 XP

Loot Tally

All the loot is pooled together, and each PC gets XP based on the amount of loot they brought back. Here however any excess loot is not lost, but the total amount is added to the Hoard. (TDB: There should probably be some that goes to the Gang Hoard, and some that goes to the Lair Hoard, like a tax)

(TDB: Is this amount reasonable?)

Loot Value XP
1-19 0 XP
20-39 1 XP
40-59 2 XP
60-79 3 XP
80+ 4 XP

Infamy Update

Based on the amount of loot and raid points, the Gangs Infamy is updated. (TDB: How much?) Perhaps it should be updated by the level of the PC instead?

Gang Actions

Gang Actions should be about getting more benefits for the gang. It could be things like expading Turf, decorating their base (for some reason) to make the Gang more attractive to other Gobbos to join them.

Lair Upgrades

Lair upgrads are big purchases, that require a lot of loot. They are permanent upgrades to the Lair, and can be used by all Gobbos in the Gang. This chould be a Mushrooom Farm, that gives them a steady supply of healing mushrooms. Or a training ground, that gives them a bonus to their training.

THis section will be a bit Boardgamey, but I think it will be fun. They unlock new buildings, and can now buy new upgrades. This is one way to make them stronger as they go. Some basebuildings like blacksmith or brewery could probably be possible be build with just loot, but other things might require some quests to be completed first. Or specific things. Like can them tame some wolfs, and build a wolf pen to then get wolfs fot the mob, so we have wolf rider mobs.

TODO: How do make this not be a slog and just bookkeeping, but a fun mechanic to get involved in?

Things to do in town?

It is a Roleplaying game after all. There should be things to do in town, and the players should be able to interact with the world. Go to a Goblin bar, and get into a fight, or hear rumors. Perhaps even have some classic PC only adventure, a side quest if you will.

Preparing for the next raid

In this section, the player can prepare gear and mobs for the next raid. In this I want to be able to outfit the mobs using loot from the hoard. So that Loot has a value more than just XP. So say that you have a Loot of 40, and you want to equip your boys with leather armor. That costs 1 loot per mobsize. So for a 3d6 mob, you need to spend 3 loot. Or similar. Here the players need to make some decisions, and we need to balance it out. Good equipment should be expensive, and dead gobbo mobs means that the equipment is lost and broken (perhaps some can be salvaged, but seems cleaner to just have it lost). So, if they man up with expensive stuff, the raid goes south the will come back with nothing, and the next raid will be harder. They they might need to grind for a while to get back on their feet.

I’m looking this a bit like they have to sell it to the Gobbos thaty they need to join on the raid. Like it was a Viking raid, where promise of loot and glory was enough to get the boys on board. Probably they need to pay them a bit as well, especially if the want to have some more experinenced Gobbos on the raid. (TODO: Do we have different levelns of Mobs? How would that playout? Perhaps via Tricks or something that can give some boons.) If they are cheap, they can get a lot of weak Gobbos, but they will die fast. If they are rich, they can get a few strong Gobbos, but they will be expensive. Perhaps they can even with their Mouth skill sell in the adventure to the Gobbos, like “If you join me on this raid, I promise you that you will get a share of the loot!” And if the raid then fail, they are in trouble with the Gobbos. Probably they should have a base set of Gang Gobbos that they can bring with them at no cost, but it need to be balanced as well somehow. Need to set this economy…

6. Creative Genius Additions (The Spice!)

Role: Creative Genius

The foundation here is excellent—it gives the game a fantastic pacing loop (Raid -> Tally -> Upgrade -> Pitch the next Raid). Let’s inject some goblin chaos into these systems to make sure it never feels like a spreadsheet!

A. The “Pitch” Mechanic (Mouth vs. Wallet)

You mentioned selling the adventure to the Mobs like a Viking raid. This is gold.

  • The Idea: Before a raid, the Boss has to literally stand on a box and pitch the raid to the Grunts.
  • The Mechanic: You can either pay them up front from the Hoard (safe, but expensive), or you can make a Mouth Test to promise them absurd riches (cheap, but dangerous).
  • The Catch: If you promised them the moon with a Mouth Test and the raid fails, the Mob Mutiny gauge maxes out instantly, or they steal from your personal Hoard while you sleep. It creates a hilarious risk/reward where charismatic Bosses can run zero-budget raids but face lethal consequences if they fail.

B. The Loot “Skim” (The Tax)

You noted that some loot should go to the Lair and some to the Gang. Goblins are greedy, so the transfer of wealth shouldn’t be peaceful.

  • The Idea: The “Lair Tax” isn’t an Excel formula; it’s a physical gauntlet.
  • The Mechanic: When the Gangs return, the Lair Boss (an NPC or an Elder) demands their cut. The players can choose to “Skim”—hiding the best loot from the tax collectors. If they succeed on a Slink test, they keep 100% of the value for their own Hoard. If they fail, the Lair guards beat them up, take the item anyway, and the Gang loses Infamy for getting caught.

C. “Dead Gobbo” XP & The Glory of Dying

You asked if requiring a PC to survive to get Raid XP is too punishing. Yes, it might encourage players to play too safely, which is un-goblin-like!

  • The Idea: Reward dying gloriously.
  • The Mechanic: If a Boss dies during the raid, their Gang still gets the XP from the Raid Tally, and they actually get a +1 XP Bonus for providing an entertaining story for the survivors to tell at the bar. This guarantees the player’s new Boss starts off stronger and softens the blow of death, encouraging reckless, fun play.

D. Goblin Bar Fights & Rumor Mill (Town Activities)

You mentioned wanting side activities so it isn’t just bookkeeping.

  • The Idea: The “Downtime Action” system (like from Wicked Ones or Blades). Each Boss gets 1 or 2 Actions in the Lair Phase.
  • The Activities:
    • Start a Bar Fight: Test Tough. Winner gains +1 Infamy and steals D6 Loot from the loser. Loser takes 1 Damage before the next raid even starts.
    • Listen for Rumors: Pay 2 Loot to the bartender to learn the layout of the next dungeon or the weakness of a monster.
    • Tape Junk Together (Crafting): Try to make a bomb out of garbage. Roll Brains. On a 1, it blows up in your face.

E. Mob “Veterans” (Mob Levels)

You asked how different levels of Mobs would play out.

  • The Idea: Don’t track individual Grunt XP (too much math). Instead, if a Mob survives a raid, they gain a “Veteran” trait.
  • The Mechanic: Give them a random, highly specific quirk/boon. Example: “Survived the Fire Trap.” Boon: Ignore the first point of fire damage. However, they now demand double the Loot/pay to go on the next raid because they know what they are doing. If you get a hyper-elite Boss with an expensive Veteran Mob, losing them is a monumental, hilarious tragedy.

Mob Combat - OSR Design Advice

When designing rules for “mob combat” with an Old-School Renaissance (OSR) or B/X D&D philosophy, the primary goal is speed, player agency, and logical adjudication over complex simulation.

Here is advice based on successful OSR TTRPG design patterns:

1. Abstraction and Scaling

OSR combat design avoids tracking individual hit points for a swarm.

  • The Single Unit: Treat 10 or 20 weaker creatures as a single, more powerful entity stat-wise. A mob of 10 goblins might just be a single “Goblin Mob” monster with 5 Hit Dice.
  • Thresholds: As the mob takes damage, its numbers dwindle. You don’t need to track exactly which goblin dies, just describe them falling as the HP goes down.

2. Simplified Attack Rolls

To speed up play, eliminate rolling 10 individual d20s.

  • Guaranteed Hits: Based on the mob’s size and the target’s Armor Class, determine a set number of automatic hits. (e.g., if 10 goblins attack AC 15, assume 3 hit automatically without rolling).
  • Aggregate Damage Calculation: Calculate the average damage output for a unit based on the individual creature’s damage and apply it directly.
  • One-Roll Resolution: Have the entire mob roll a single d20. On a hit, they deal damage scaled to their current size/HP.

3. Morale is Critical

  • Morale Checks: This is vital in OSR mob combat. When an enemy unit suffers significant losses (e.g., first casualty, losing half their numbers), they must roll a Morale check (usually 2d6 under their Morale score). If they fail, the mob breaks and flees. You rarely fight a mob to the last man.

4. Player Impact and Rulings

  • Rulings, Not Rules: Allow players to use the environment, clever traps, or fire to break the mob’s formation instead of just swinging swords.
  • Focus on the Goal: Mob combat is often an obstacle to a goal, not a fight to the death. Give players a way to route the mob by taking out its leader or using loud noises, emphasizing player skill over character statistics.

Researcher Report: Crafting Systems in TTRPGs

Topic: Crafting systems that are easy, fun, highly customizable, emphasize scrap hunting over monetary purchases, and involve “fun matching” to create new gear.

Executive Summary

Historically, crafting in TTRPGs falls into two traps: it is either too abstracted (just roll a die and subtract gold) or overly complex (requiring spreadsheets of distinct reagents like “3x Iron, 1x Wolf Pelt”).

For a game emphasizing chaotic, “scrap-built” ingenuity—perfect for goblins—the best systems abstract the materials into generic “Scrap” or “Parts,” but emphasize the components’ traits to allow for “fun matching” and combinatorial creativity.

Below is an analysis of systems that successfully balance simplicity with deep, rewarding customization.

1. Break!! RPG (The “Fun Matching” Gold Standard)

The Mechanic: Break!! has a brilliantly simple crafting/upgrading system where you combine two distinct objects to create a new one. The newly forged item inherits both the advantages and the disadvantages (or quirks) of the components used.

  • Why it works: It requires zero complex math or material tracking.
  • Scrap Hunting: Players hunt for items with specific, interesting tags rather than raw crafting materials.
  • Goblin Application: Taping a “Heavy Hammer” to a “Firecracker” yields a “Heavy Firecracker Hammer” that does massive damage but might explode in your hands (inheriting the volatile nature of the firecracker). It directly gamifies the “fun matching.”

2. Mutant: Year Zero (The “Scrap Survival” Standard)

The Mechanic: Players explore the post-apocalyptic Zone and scavenge exactly what it sounds like: Scrap. The “Gearhead” class uses this generic Scrap to jury-rig temporary, highly effective weapons and vehicles.

  • Why it works: It forces players to constantly hunt for junk. Equipment degrades over time and breaks, meaning players are trapped in a constant loop of finding scrap and building new, temporary solutions.
  • Scrap Hunting: Scrap is the primary currency and lifeline of the game. You don’t buy things; you build them from garbage.
  • Goblin Application: Goblins building crude, temporary death-machines out of garbage fits perfectly. A system where weapons naturally degrade on natural 1s, requiring more scrap to repair or invent a new replacement, creates a continuous cycle of scavenging.

3. Index Card RPG / ICRPG (The “MacGyver” Approach)

The Mechanic: ICRPG focuses heavily on “Loot” rather than character stats. The crafting system usually boils down to the GM and player agreeing on combining two pieces of Loot, requiring a certain amount of “Effort” (a mechanical track) to complete the construction.

  • Why it works: It is entirely narrative but mechanically grounded by the “Effort” system (e.g., this crazy invention takes “2 Hearts” of crafting effort to finish, meaning the player has to spend actions during downtime or even mid-combat to assemble it).
  • Goblin Application: It gives a mechanical pacing to crafting. Building a “Scrap-apult” mid-combat takes 3 successful crafting actions, giving the rest of the mob time to defend the crafter.

4. Numenera / Cypher System (The “Weird Tech” Approach)

The Mechanic: The world is littered with “Cyphers” (one-time use, highly specific pieces of magical/technological junk). The Numenera Destiny supplement introduced a crafting system based on “Iotum”—abstracted salvage like “Io” or “Quantium” used to build installations.

  • Why it works: Cyphers force players to use weird combinations of items because they can only carry a few at a time. If they tape an “Anti-gravity emitter” to a “Plasma cell,” weird things happen.
  • Goblin Application: Treating “Scrap” not just as metal, but as weird, volatile components (e.g., “Shiny Rock,” “Bouncy Fungus,” “Angry Bug”) allows players to mix and match to see what chaotic effect occurs.

Synthesis & Recommendations for “Gobbos”

Based on the research, a “not overly complex, fun matching, scrap-hunting” system should embrace the following framework:

A. Abstract the Currency, Specify the Tags

Do not track “wood” vs “iron.” Instead, players scavenge “Scrap” (the generic fuel for crafting) and “Oddities/Bits” (unique items that provide specific Tags).

  • Example: A player finds 3 Scrap and 1 “Bouncy” Oddity.

B. The “Duct Tape” Rule (From Break!!)

Crafting should literally be combining two items/tags to generate a new item with both properties.

  • Recipe: Base Item + Oddity + [X] Scrap = New Toy.
  • Example: Base “Choppa” (Damage 1) + “Bouncy” Oddity + 2 Scrap = “Bouncy Choppa” (Can be thrown and bounces back, but might hit the user on a fail).

C. Volatility and Degradation (From Mutant: Year Zero)

Goblin tech shouldn’t last forever. Powerful crafted combinations should have a risk of breaking down, forcing the goblins to continually hunt for more scrap rather than hoarding a perfect weapon forever.

Output Summary

The most successful systems for this specific niche avoid deep simulation. They provide players with a sandbox of descriptive tags and a generic currency (scrap/effort), trusting the players and GM to organically create chaotic, unique, and highly breakable equipment.

Role: The Researcher (Workflow Output)

Topic: TTRPG Mechanics for Acquiring Abilities via “Deeds”, Specific Actions, or Achievements. Objective: Identify, compare, and analyze TTRPGs that utilize achievement-based progression or specific action-triggers to award abilities, and provide objective commentary on their application to the chaotic philosophy of Gobbos.

Findings: Deed-Based Progression in TTRPGs

The concept of tying specific mechanical rewards to specific in-game actions is an established, albeit niche, mechanic in TTRPG design. It is often employed to enforce genre tropes, encourage proactive player behavior, and replace traditional, math-heavy Experience Point (XP) systems.

1. The “Beats” System (Heart: The City Beneath & Spire)

  • Mechanic: Characters advance not by killing monsters, but by fulfilling “Beats”. These are specific narrative or mechanical goals chosen by the player (e.g., “Take major fallout,” “Kill a significant enemy,” “Perform a blood sacrifice”).
  • Award: Achieving a Minor, Major, or Zenith Beat immediately grants an advance of the corresponding tier (a specific new ability or feature).
  • Commentary: This is highly relevant to Gobbos. By putting the “Deed” on the player’s character sheet as a prerequisite for advancement, it incentivizes the players to drive the plot and seek out those exact chaotic situations. It is a proactive, rather than reactive, progression system.

2. The “Scar” Table (Into the Odd & Electric Bastionland)

  • Mechanic: When a character is reduced to exactly 0 HP, they roll on a “Scars” table. The result is tied to the specific type of trauma they endured.
  • Award: They might gain a weird new ability, a permanent physical alteration (which could have mechanical benefits), or a permanent change to their stats.
  • Commentary: This aligns perfectly with the proposed “Die by Fire” or “Die because of a Fumble” deeds. It turns severe failure or near-death experiences into avenues for gaining unique Tricks, reinforcing the roguelite, lethal nature of Gobbos.

3. Condition & Obsession Mechanics (Unknown Armies & Changeling: The Lost)

  • Mechanic: In Unknown Armies, spellcasters (Adepts) gain magical “charges” (the fuel for their abilities) by performing highly specific, often self-destructive or bizarre deeds (e.g., reckless driving, smoking, physical self-harm).
  • Award: The explicit fuel needed to use their most powerful Tricks.
  • Commentary: While this is for fueling rather than acquiring abilities, the design philosophy is identical. It mechanically demands chaotic roleplaying.

4. Achievement & Destiny Milestones (Level Up: Advanced 5E & Heroic Deeds RPG)

  • Mechanic: Characters have stated “Destinies” or specific “Deeds” tied to their archetype. Completing a set number of these specific achievements triggers a level-up.
  • Award: Broader character advancement or specific archetype features.
  • Commentary: This is a more traditional milestone approach. It is less modular than acquiring a single Trick for a single Deed, making it perhaps too rigid for a fast-paced Goblin game, but it validates the core concept of achievement-driven play.

Alternatives & Pedantic Recommendations for Gobbos

If Gobbos implements “Deeds” to acquire “Tricks”, the following implementation structures present the most robust alternatives:

  1. The Prerequisite Model (The “Beat” Approach): List the Tricks, and beside each, list the “Deed” required to unlock it. Example: Trick: Pyromaniac’s Joy | Deed: Survive being set on fire, or set an enemy on fire while you are also on fire. Why it’s better: Players can read the rulebook, find a Trick they want, and spend the session trying to orchestrate the chaotic event required to get it.

  2. The Death/Lineage Model (The “Roguelite” Approach): Since death is a core tenet of Gobbos leveling, tie the Deeds to the manner of the previous character’s death or the pinnacle achievement of their short life. Example: If a Gobbo dies by ordering a Mob beyond their Grunt capacity (leading to a Mob mutiny), the next Gobbo the player rolls up instantly starts with the “Authoritarian” Trick. Why it’s better: It perfectly honors Design Tenet #5 (Character death is a part of leveling up) and softens the blow of losing a character by rewarding the player for how spectacularly they failed.

Link Library (Reliable Sources & Free Rules)

Note: Direct AnyFlip download links are frequently obfuscated by search engines or removed for copyright reasons. However, official free Quickstarts and SRDs are readily available for these games.

  • Rowan, Rook and Decard - Heart: The City Beneath
    • Free PDF: Heart Quickstart Rules (DriveThruRPG / Itch.io)
    • Comment: The industry standard for the “Beats” progression system. Highly recommended reading for structuring proactive, player-driven achievement goals that grant abilities.
  • Rowan, Rook and Decard - Spire: The City Must Fall
    • Free PDF: Spire Quickstart PDF
    • Comment: Shares the exact “Beats” advancement system with Heart.
  • Bastionland - Electric Bastionland
    • Free PDF: Electric Bastionland Free Edition
    • Comment: A masterclass in OSR fast-resolution and lethality. The “Scars” system is the definitive source for rewarding players with cool abilities for surviving terrible, specific trauma. The Free Edition contains the core rules.
  • Bastionland - Into the Odd Remastered
    • Free PDF: Mark of the Odd SRD / Free Version (Often hosted on Bastionland blog)
    • Comment: The precursor to Electric Bastionland, featuring rapid character creation and deadly, consequence-based mechanics.
  • Heroic Deeds RPG System
    • Comment: A rules-lite system that entirely replaces XP with specific “Deeds”. Good for analyzing how to balance a game entirely around achievements rather than math-based progression.
  • Level Up: Advanced 5E
    • Free Rules: A5E SRD / Tools site
    • Comment: Has an officially open rule system online, which details “Destinies” (achievements that unlock specific abilities for characters).

Researcher Findings: Base & Lair Building Mechanics

Role: The Researcher Objective: Analyze the best mechanical implementations of base/domain building in existing TTRPGs to inspire the “Lair Phase” in Gobbos.

Top Contenders & Mechanics

1. Blades in the Dark (Forged in the Dark)

Blades in the Dark treats the criminal Crew and its Lair as a shared player-character.

  • The “Claims” Map (Turf Expansion): Instead of just buying upgrades from a list, the Lair has a visual web/map of “Claims” (e.g., Fighting Pits, Smuggling Route, Informant Network). You start in the center. To get an upgrade, you have to do a raid to conquer an adjacent node on the map.
    • Gobbo Application: Instead of just paying “Loot” for a Blacksmith, the Gangs look at a Lair Map and say “We need to clear out the Flooded Caves node to build a Mushroom Farm.” It turns base building into a physical turf war.
  • Crew Upgrades: Modular, checklist-style upgrades to the physical Lair (Vaults to hold more wealth, Quarters to heal faster).

2. Mutant: Year Zero (Free League)

This game features “The Ark,” a central hub where the post-apocalyptic survivors live.

  • Collaborative “Projects”: During the Ark Phase, all players vote on a collective Project (e.g., building a Palisade, a Water Purifier, or a Defensible Gate). Players then assign their characters to work on it during Downtime to advance a “Progress Clock.”
  • Development Levels (DEV): The Ark has four stats: Food Supply, Culture, Technology, and Warfare. Completing Projects raises these stats. You cannot build advanced Projects (like a Generator) until the Ark’s Technology stat is high enough.
    • Gobbo Application: The Lair itself has “Dev Levels.” If the Gangs want advanced weapons from the shop, they must collectively raise the Lair’s “Scrap-Tech” level first.

3. Wicked Ones

A game where players are specifically playing monsters running a dungeon. Highly relevant.

  • Lurking vs. Roaming Phases: Hard mechanical distinction between being in the dungeon (Lurking) and going on raids (Roaming).
  • Dungeon Discoveries: As players spend downtime strictly “digging” to expand their lair to build new rooms, the GM rolls for Discoveries. They might unearth an underground river, a hostile insect hive, or raw minerals.
    • Gobbo Application: Expanding the Lair isn’t just spending money; it’s manual labor that can accidentally trigger mini-encounters right inside the home base.
  • Downtime Action Economy: Players get exactly 2 Downtime actions to spend on Lair upkeep, trap building, or brewing potions.

4. Forbidden Lands (Free League)

Focuses on OSR-style hexcrawling and taking over an empty castle to build a “Stronghold.”

  • Functions & Hirelings: You can build physical “Functions” in your Stronghold (e.g., a Forge, a Mill, a Guard Tower). However, the Function does nothing unless you assign a specific NPC Hireling to run it (a Smith, a Miller, a Guard Captain).
    • Gobbo Application: This perfectly validates our brainstormed “Elder” mechanic. A Lair Upgrade is useless unless a player officially retires their Boss as an Elder and assigns them to run the facility.

Synthesis & Recommendations for Gobbos

If we want the Lair to be easy to use but mechanically rich, we should steal the following framework:

  1. The Phase Split (Wicked Ones): Hard sequence of play. The Raid Phase (Action) -> The Lair Phase (Base Building/Upkeep/Healing).
  2. The Claims Web (Blades in the Dark): The Lair Upgrades shouldn’t just be a spreadsheet. It should be a visual “Tech Tree” or Map. To unlock the Goblin Catapult, you must first unlock the Scrap Yard node.
  3. The Hireling Tax (Forbidden Lands): To permanently activate a major node on the Claims Web, a Boss must be retired into an Elder to run it. This creates a fantastic economy where players must choose between keeping a Level 6 Boss for personal glory, or retiring them to give the whole table a massive Lair upgrade.

Source Links (For Further Reading)

  • Blades in the Dark SRD (Crew & Lair): https://bladesinthedark.com/crew
  • Wicked Ones (Free Edition available on DriveThruRPG): An excellent resource for monster-focused downtime.
  • Note: I did not need to use AnyFlip for this research as the core mechanical loops of these systems are widely documented and openly available in SRDs or designer breakdowns online.

Researcher Findings: Common TTRPG Conditions & Status Effects

Role: The Researcher Objective: Analyze the most common and essential status conditions used in major TTRPGs (like D&D 5e and Pathfinder 2e) to provide a curated list for the Gobbos system.

The Consensus: The “Top 8” Essential Conditions

Across the most popular tactical TTRPGs, the following conditions form the mechanical backbone of combat debuffs. I have grouped them thematically and suggested how they might cleanly map to Gobbos’ existing Mechanics (Tough, Slink, Brains, Mouth, Notice, and Movement).

1. Blinded / Dazzled (Sensory)

  • How it works elsewhere: Disadvantage/Penalties on attacks; enemies get advantage against the blinded target. Auto-fail on sight-based checks.
  • Gobbo Application: You already have this listed (-1 on any Physical tests and Notice). Very standard and universally applied.

2. Restrained / Grappled (Mobility)

  • How it works elsewhere: Speed drops to 0. Penalties to Dexterity (Slink) and attacks.
  • Gobbo Application: You have Restrained as -1d on Slink tests. You should also explicitly state that their Movement becomes 0 (or they cannot Move as a Standard Action).

3. Frightened / Terrified (Morale)

  • How it works elsewhere: Cannot willingly move closer to the source of fear. Penalties to all checks/attacks while in line of sight of the source.
  • Gobbo Application: A goblin staple. Terrified: “Cannot move closer to the source of fear. -1d to all attacks/tests against the source.”

4. Prone (Positioning)

  • How it works elsewhere: Being knocked down. Costs movement to stand up. Melee attacks against the prone target have an advantage; ranged attacks have a disadvantage (because they are a smaller target).
  • Gobbo Application: Prone: “Costs a Standard Action (or 1 Movement) to stand up. Enemies get +1d to melee attacks against you.”

5. Poisoned / Sickened (Biological)

  • How it works elsewhere: Flat penalty/disadvantage to all attacks and ability checks. Sometimes causes persistent damage at the end of a turn.
  • Gobbo Application: Poisoned: “-1d on all Tough and Slink tests” OR “Take 1 Damage at the end of your turn until you pass a Tough test.”

6. Stunned / Incapacitated (Action Economy)

  • How it works elsewhere: The harshest condition. The creature cannot take actions or reactions, cannot move, and automatically fails physical saves.
  • Gobbo Application: Stunned: “You lose your next Standard Action. Enemies get +1d to attack you.” (Keep it simple since Gobbos action economy is precious).

7. Exhausted / Enfeebled (Fatigue)

  • How it works elsewhere: Often a tiered system (D&D) or flat penalty to Strength (Pathfinder).
  • Gobbo Application: Weakened (which you have): -1d on Tough tests. This perfectly covers the exhaustion/enfeebled archetype.

8. Flat-Footed / Off-Guard (Defense/Distracted)

  • How it works elsewhere: Represents being flanked or surprised. Lowers Armor Class / Defense.
  • Gobbo Application: Confused (which you have): -1d on Brains tests. We could expand “Distracted/Off-Guard” to mean enemies roll +1d to attack you because your guard is down.

The Gobbos Condition List (Review of your drafted list)

Your current list is very clean because it directly targets the core stats:

  • Weakened (-1d Tough) - Great.
  • Restrained (-1d Slink) - Great, just add “Movement becomes 0.”
  • Confused (-1d Brains) - Great.
  • Silenced (-1d Mouth) - Great, very funny for goblin spellcasters or commanders.
  • Blinded (-1 on Physical/Notice) - Great.
  • Deafened (-1 on Notice) - Great.

What to fill in:

  • Poisoned: Suggest either persistent damage (e.g., lose 1 Grit/Grunt per turn) OR a flat -1d to all Combat actions.
  • Terrified: Suggest positional control (e.g., “Must spend your Move action running away from the source, or -1d on all actions”).
  • (Missing but Recommended) Prone: Highly recommend adding Prone, as getting knocked down in a chaotic goblin fight is a classic trope.
  • (Missing but Recommended) Stunned: Missing a turn is a big deal, but necessary for heavy blows.

Does this list align with the level of complexity you want? If so, I can draft the exact rules text for Poisoned, Terrified, Prone, and Stunned to insert directly into 07_Wounds_Conditions.md!

Researcher Findings: Optimal OSR Frameworks for Gobbos

Objective: To analyze and recommend tabletop roleplaying game (TTRPG) frameworks that align with the design goals of “Gobbos”—specifically, a requirement for high lethality, fast resolution, ruling-over-rules mechanics, and a focus on inventory and retainers over complex character sheets or narrative superpowers.

1. The Core Problem with Modern Fantasy (e.g., D&D 5e)

As initially hypothesized, modern narrative fantasy systems are fundamentally incompatible with the “Goblin” experience. These systems prioritize:

  • Heroic attrition: Characters have massive health pools and are expected to survive 6-8 encounters per day.
  • Action bloat: Turns take too long due to bonus actions, reactions, and complex spellcasting.
  • Inherent power: Characters gain power intrinsically through leveling, rather than extrinsically through loot or clever environmental interaction. Goblins, by their nature, should be fragile, cowardly, and reliant on numbers, dirty tricks, and scrounged gear.

2. The Solution: Classless, Inventory-Driven OSR

To properly model the “Gobbos” experience—where Mob combat, Orders, and Loot capacity are paramount—we must look to the minimalist branches of the Old-School Renaissance (OSR).

A. Knave (by Ben Milton)

  • Key Mechanic: There are no classes. Your character’s role is entirely defined by their inventory. A goblin holding a wand is a wizard; a goblin holding a shield is a tank.
  • Loot Capacity: Knave has an exceptionally elegant, slot-based inventory system tied to the Constitution stat. This directly solves the “Loot capacity” design goal for Gobbos. Pushing limits (carrying heavy loot) actively penalizes the character’s ability to survive.
  • Verdict: Highly Recommended. The slot-based inventory is the perfect engine for greedy goblins hauling junk.

B. Into the Odd / Cairn (by Chris McDowall / Yochai Gal)

  • Key Mechanic: Combat completely bypasses the “to-hit” roll. If an attack is made, it automatically deals damage, subtracted by armor.
  • Lethality: Because damage is guaranteed, combat is extremely fast and terrifyingly lethal. Players learn to avoid fair fights at all costs—the quintessential goblin strategy.
  • Verdict: Recommended for Combat Engine. The auto-damage mechanic perfectly models chaotic, brutal Mob combat, cutting down dice rolling by half.

C. Mörk Borg (by Ockull and Nohr)

(Reference PDF successfully acquired via Anyflip)

  • Key Mechanic: A brutally rules-light system where the world is actively ending. It utilizes a “player-facing” roll system; the Game Master never rolls dice for monster attacks or defenses (players roll to attack, and roll to defend).
  • Morale: Highly developed morale tables for enemies (and retainers), dictating when they break and run.
  • Verdict: Recommended for Tone and Player-Facing Mechanics. Having the GM never roll dice speeds up Mob combat significantly, allowing the GM to focus entirely on narration.

3. Secondary OSR Concepts (Basic Fantasy, Dolmenwood, Land of Eem)

While direct Anyflip PDFs for these specific games are tightly controlled or unavailable, their design philosophies offer further inspiration:

A. Basic Fantasy RPG / Old School Essentials (OSE) / Old D&D

  • Key Mechanic: Strict B/X D&D recreation. Race-as-class (in OSE/Old D&D) or simplified race/class splits (Basic Fantasy).
  • The Grind: These systems emphasize the “dungeon turn” (tracking torches, wandering monsters, and rations strictly).
  • Verdict: Excellent for structural pacing. We should ensure Gobbos has a strict tracker for resource depletion to keep the pressure high.

B. Dolmenwood

  • Key Mechanic: A deeply integrated hex-crawl built on top of OSE rules, focusing on localized flavor, fairy tales, and specific regional Gangs over generic fantasy.
  • Verdict: A great reminder that the “flavor” of the world matters. The “Mob Flavors” in Gobbos (Banes and Boons) should feel intensely grounded in goblin lore rather than generic fantasy tropes.

C. Land of Eem / Oathhammer

  • Key Mechanic (Land of Eem): Lighthearted, whimsical TTRPG designed around simple resolutions and narrative fun.
  • Verdict: Goblin games need comedy! “Order Fumbles” and hilarious failures should be a core mechanic to break up the lethality.

4. Recommended Amalgamation for Gobbos

Based on objective analysis, Gobbos should adopt:

  1. Inventory-as-Class (from Knave): Ditch standard character sheets in favor of a slot-based inventory grid.
  2. Auto-Damage or Player-Facing Rolls (from Into the Odd / Mörk Borg): Streamline Mob combat by removing the GM’s need to roll d20s for every attacking enemy.
  3. Strict Morale (from B/X D&D): Retainers (Grunts) and enemies must constantly test morale. Goblins are not fearless; they run away. The “Orders” mechanic should directly interact with a Grunt’s Morale score.

Researcher Findings: Optimal OSR Source Material

Objective: The user requested further research into acquiring specific OSR (Old-School Renaissance) rulebooks and documents, specifically providing the example of Moldvay B/X D&D hosted on AnyFlip (https://anyflip.com/inssi/oitr/basic). The goal is to provide reliable, accessible sources to inspire the “Gobbos” system’s mechanics (Mob combat, Orders, Morale).

1. Analysis of AnyFlip and Document Sharing Sites

  • The AnyFlip Example: The provided URL https://anyflip.com/inssi/oitr/basic successfully links to an uploaded scan of the 1981 Dungeons & Dragons Basic Box Set (often called the Moldvay Basic rules).
  • The Problem with Document Hubs: While AnyFlip, FlipHTML5, and VK occasionally host these documents, they suffer from three major drawbacks for a game designer:
    1. Obfuscation: Uploaders intentionally disguise names (e.g., “DND bx” instead of the actual title) to avoid copyright takedowns, making targeted searching extremely difficult.
    2. Usability: Scanned PDFs hosted in flip-book viewers cannot be easily searched (Ctrl+F), excerpted, or copy-pasted into your own design notes.
    3. Legality & Reliability: Links frequently suffer from DMCA takedowns and rot, meaning you might lose access to your reference material mid-design.

2. The Researcher’s Recommendation: Official Free SRDs

As a Researcher, my job is to find the best practice and most reliable sources. Fortunately, the OSR movement is predominantly open-source. Instead of relying on obfuscated AnyFlip links, there are vastly superior, 100% legal, profoundly accessible, and free alternatives that contain the exact mechanics requested.

Strategy 1: The “B/X D&D” Foundation (For Morale & Grunts)

Rather than reading a scanned 1981 PDF on AnyFlip, you should use modern retro-clones. These games mathematically recreate the exact B/X rules but format them for modern readers.

  • Old-School Essentials (OSE) SRD:
    • Source: oldschoolessentials.necroticgnome.com/srd
    • Why it’s better: This is the current gold standard. It is a completely free, hyperlinked, searchable wiki containing all B/X D&D rules. It perfectly outlines Morale rules (2d6 checks) and Retainer/Mercenary (Grunt) rules.
  • Basic Fantasy RPG:
    • Source: basicfantasy.org/downloads.html
    • Why it’s better: The entire game system and dozens of community expansions are available as free high-quality PDF downloads on their official site. It streamlines the Moldvay B/X rules for faster play.

Strategy 2: Modular Rules-Light Systems (For Fast Combat & Brutality)

If the goal is to find games with blazing fast combat (auto-hits) and modular “inventory-as-class” mechanics for Gobbos, these systems are given away for free by their creators.

  • Cairn RPG (First & Second Edition):
    • Source: cairnrpg.com/cairn-srd
    • Why it’s better: Cairn combines the inventory system of Knave with the auto-hit, incredibly fast combat of Into the Odd. The entire text is CC-BY-SA 4.0 licensed, meaning the HTML rules are completely free on their website.

3. Conclusion & Next Steps

Stop searching AnyFlip. While it worked as a one-off for the Mörk Borg or B/X PDF, you will spend more time fighting the search engine than designing your game.

Recommendation for Gobbos Design:

  1. Open the OSE SRD, navigate strictly to the Encounters and Retainers sections, and copy their 2d6 Morale mechanics to finalize the Orders system.
  2. Open the Cairn RPG SRD, read the Combat section (which features auto-damage), and use this as the baseline for your Mob Combat interactions to keep goblin fights terrifyingly fast.

Inspiration Notes (Updated for OSR focus)

As requested, I have pivoted from modern narrative games like 5e and looked into Old-School Renaissance (OSR) and B/X D&D-style reference materials. These systems focus on fast, deadly combat, rulings over rules, and resource management. Here is what is relevant for Gobbos:

1. Basic Fantasy RPG / Old-School Essentials (OSE)

Why it’s interesting:

  • OSR Simplicity: These systems strip back the rules to create fast, dangerous combat. Their mechanics are an excellent template for Gobbos.
  • Retainer/Grunt Mechanics: OSR features standard rules for hiring retainers (“Grunts”), tracking their morale, and detailing how they follow orders. Morale checks specifically (usually rolling 2d6 under a morale score) dictate if units break or flee when hit, which is perfect for “Orders” in Gobbos.
  • Lethality: Goblins are famously fragile, and OSR games excel at presenting extremely dangerous environments where low-level characters die easily if they don’t use tactics or gang up on enemies.

2. OSR Mob Combat Design Advice

Why it’s interesting:

  • A compiled summary of OSR community best practices for mass combat (such as the old BECMI “War Machine” or fan-made One-Roll systems).
  • Highlights include treating a mob as a single hit die entity, simplifying attack rolls (e.g., auto-hits based on numbers), and heavily relying on Reaction and Morale rolls instead of just hit-point attrition.
  • These principles should directly inspire the “Mob Flavors” and how Mobs take damage and flee in Gobbos!

Alternate Systems (Honorable Mentions)

  • Wicked Ones (Free Edition): A Forged in the Dark game where you play as the monsters building a dungeon. Great inspiration for monster-centric play.
  • Goblin Quest: A slapstick RPG specifically about Goblins trying to accomplish fatal tasks. Good for looking at “Order Fumbles” and hilarious consequence tables.
  • Mausritter: Contains excellent “Warband” rules where squads of mice fight as single units using very streamlined OSR rules.

Rules Lawyer Audit: Equipment Framework Brainstorm

Subject: 00_DEV_Brainstorms/Equipment_Framework_Brainstorm.md Auditor: The Rules Lawyer ([@/rules_lawyer](file:///c:/Users/ante/Documents/github/gobbos/.agent/workflows/rules_lawyer.md))

1. Terminology & Keywords Check

  • “Reaction / Free” vs Action Economy: The draft uses “Reaction / Free” for items usable instantly.
    • Conflict: “Free” is used in 10_Stats.md specifically for “Free Orders” (granted by Mouth), which are still your turn actions, just not costing standard actions. “Reaction” implies an out-of-turn trigger (like being attacked). If an item can be used out of turn, “Reaction” is the correct term. If it doesn’t cost an action but must be done on your turn (like drinking a potion), it needs a distinct keyword to prevent confusing it with Free Orders.
    • Recommendation: Standardize the wording. Use Reaction for out-of-turn triggered effects. Use Incidental or Minor Action for things on your turn that don’t cost a full action.
  • “Passive Defence”:
    • Status: Corrected. 10_Stats.md explicitly calls it “Passive Dodge Dice”. However, the brainstorm document now says “Passive Defence”.
    • Conflict: 10_Stats.md explicitly uses “Passive Dodge Dice”. Changing it to “Passive Defence” in the Equipment document creates a terminology mismatch. If the rule is renaming it to “Passive Defence”, then 10_Stats.md MUST be updated from “Passive Dodge Dice” to “Passive Defence”.
  • “Value”:
    • Status: Clarified. It functions as currency. No direct conflicts with existing rules, though “Loot” in 12_Gang.md is tied to Renown. The framework aligns well by linking Value to Lair Upgrades and Renown.

2. Rules Consistency & Conflicts

  • Carry Capacity (Bulk constraint):
    • Consistency: Matches 10_Stats.md perfectly. Carry Capacity is derived from Tough. No conflicts.
  • Weapons modifying Dice Pools:
    • Consistency: Matches the core mechanic in 01_Dice.md where equipment adds dice to the pool. Standardizing weapons as +1d or +2d respects the “no math interpretation” tenet.
  • Ablative Armor:
    • Complexity Check: The user’s comment notes (Comment: this might be a bit complex for Mobs, but perhaps a good thing for PC). The Rules Lawyer agrees. Mobs should not have ablative armor tracking; it bogs down Mob combat. It should strictly apply to the PC Boss.
  • Named Items & Death (15_Death.md):
    • Integration Check: 15_Death.md introduces “Named Items” with minor magical traits when a Boss dies. The Equipment framework’s section on “Mechanical Traits” neatly creates space for this. Named Items simply gain a Trait. No conflicts.

3. Structural & Fast Play Assessment

  • Generic Structure: By avoiding specific weapon rules and keeping “Traits” rare, the framework succeeds in the “Slim Rules” goal.
  • Action Usage: Explicitly stating if an item takes an Action or is Passive/Reaction is excellent for fast play at the table. It prevents GM/Player arguments over action economy.

4. Verdict & Next Steps

The framework is Solid, but requires a few specific keyword cleanups before moving to STAGE_Drafts or statting items.

Required Action Items for the User/Dev:

  1. Resolve “Passive Dodge” vs “Passive Defence”: Decide on ONE term. If it’s Passive Defence, we must update 10_Stats.md (lines 17-23).
  2. Clarify “Free” Item Usage: Decide if a zero-action item used on your turn is called an “Incidental,” a “Minor Action,” or if “Free” is acceptable despite “Free Orders” existing.
  3. Lock in Ablative Armor: Add a rule specifically stating “Ablative Armor applies only to PCs, not Mobs” to avoid tracking headaches.

Brainstorm: Static Resistances & Opposed Tests

Context: Since the GM does not roll dice in Gobbos, how do “opposed” actions work? E.g. A gobbo arm-wrestling an Orc, or trying to sneak past an active guard.

The Core Idea

  • Player rolls against a static Target Number (TN).
  • The TN is generated by looking at the enemy’s relevant stat.
    • Example: If a player attacks, the TN is equal to the Enemy’s Defense rating.
    • Example: If a player arm-wrestles an Orc, the TN equals the Orc’s Toughness.
    • Example: If a player sneaks, the TN is based on the Guard’s Notice/Brains stat.

Questions to figure out

  • How does the math scale? A starting Gobbo rolls 2d6 or 3d6. If an Orc has a Toughness of 3, how hard is that really?
  • Do we convert 1:1 (Enemy Stat = TN)? Or is there a formula (e.g., Enemy Stat divided by 2)? We need to simulate the math.
  • Does the GM ever adjust Difficulty (Easy/Normal/Hard) based on the enemy, or only the TN? (Current leaning: Enemy stats dictate TN. Situational chaos dictates the Easy/Normal/Hard difficulty).

Trick Mechanics Master List

This is a brainstormed list of purely mechanical effects for Tricks, stripped of flavor text and balanced considerations, organized by category.

1. Boons & Dice Manipulation

  • Static Bonus: +1d on [Specific Action/Skill/Weapon type] (e.g., +1d on Dodge, +1d on Shooting).
  • Conditional Bonus: +1d on tests when you are at exactly 1 Grit.
  • Penalty to Enemies: Enemies get -1d when attacking you with [Ranged/Melee].
  • Rerolls: Reroll 1 failed die on a [Specific Stat] test.
  • Target Adjustment: Treat one 4 as a 5 / Treat one failure as a success on [Action].
  • Exploding Dice: If you roll a 6 on [Action], roll an additional die.
  • Crit manipulation: On a roll with multiple 5s/6s, inflict a free [Status] (e.g., Bleeding, Stunned, Prone) in addition to normal damage.
  • Success Multiplier: When attacking with [Weapon], 6s count as two successes instead of one.
  • Lowest Die Drop: Discard the lowest die result from your pool instead of accepting a failure.
  • Pushing: Take 1 Grit damage to reroll all failed dice on a crucial test.
  • Critical Shift: 5s and 6s count as critical successes instead of just 6s.
  • Fail Forward: When you fail a roll completely (no successes), gain 1 Grunt, 1 Grit, or +1d on your next test.
  • Shared Success: If you generate more successes than required for a test, grant an ally +1d on their next action.

2. Economy Bending (Actions & Time)

  • Conditional Extra Action: Do an extra action when you only have one Grit left.
  • Momentum Action: Move 1 zone as a Free Action after performing a successful Melee Attack.
  • Cleave Action: If you reduce an enemy to 0 HP, immediately make another Attack action (Limit: once per turn).
  • Free Object Interaction: Swap weapons, ready a thrown weapon, or drink a potion as a Free Action.
  • Reaction Attack: When an enemy misses you with a Melee Attack, make one Melee Attack against them as a Reaction.
  • Free Recovery: Stand up from Prone as a Free Action.
  • Action Downgrade: Perform [Specific Standard Action] (e.g., Hide/Sneak) as a Free Action once per turn.
  • Interrupt: Activate this Trick to take your turn immediately before an enemy acts in the initiative order.
  • Quick Reload: Reloading a [Specific Weapon Type] is a Free Action instead of a standard action.
  • Double Move: Move 2 zones using a single Move action, but suffer -1d to any attacks made later this turn.
  • Preparation: If you do not make any attack actions this turn, you gain an extra Standard Action on your next turn.

3. Rule Breaking & Bypassing

  • Auto-Pass: Can climb without making a Slink test.
  • Stat Swapping: Use [Stat X] instead of [Stat Y] for [Specific Action] (e.g., Roll Brains instead of Brawn to attack with an odd weapon).
  • Property Ignorance: Ignore the “Heavy” penalty on weapons.
  • Property Ignorance 2: Ignore the “Loud” property on specific weapons.
  • Constraint Breaking: You can wield a Two-Handed weapon in one hand.
  • Movement Bypassing: Move through enemy zones without triggering attacks of opportunity or movement penalties.
  • Hazard Bypassing: You do not trigger traps that rely on weight or tripwires.
  • Damage Mitigation: Ignore the first point of fall damage / ignore the first X meters of falling.
